project_overview.project_name: Somalia Urban Resilience Project II (SURP II)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To strengthen public service delivery capacity of local governments and increase access to urban infrastructure and services in selected areas.
project_overview.total_financing: US$112 million (IDA US$50 million + Somalia Multi-Partner Fund US$62 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Residents of Mogadishu (BRA), Garowe, Kismayo, and Baidoa; IDPs, returnees, and host communities; approximately 530 people from short-term employment opportunities; municipal staff from capacity building; future beneficiaries in Galmudug and Hirshabelle States

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Somalia is emerging from 2.5 decades of violent conflict and fragility. Al Shabaab retains control of territory and is present in urban areas including Mogadishu, Kismayo, and Baidoa. Mogadishu, Merca, and Kismayo have been named the world's three most fragile cities. The project specifically targets areas affected by conflict, displacement, and climate-related shocks.

key_components.description: Financing for preparation and implementation of urban infrastructure investments including roads, drainage, pedestrian walkways, street lighting, and bridges. Includes preparatory activities (feasibility studies, designs, safeguard documentation) for existing cities (Mogadishu, Garowe, Kismayo, Baidoa) and new cities in Galmudug/Hirshabelle. Infrastructure selection based on criteria including critical gaps, strategic alignment with urban resilience, inclusivity for vulnerable groups, labor intensiveness, climate resilience, and sustainability. Funding allocated in two installments with implementation readiness criteria.
key_components.financing: US$89 million (IDA US$37 million + Somalia MPF US$52 million)

key_components.description: Technical assistance on informal settlements formalization, urban infrastructure operations and maintenance, and urban governance/services (solid waste management as entry point). Includes support for inter-governmental forums and O&M planning. Aims to strengthen municipal government capacity and inter-governmental coordination.
key_components.financing: US$5 million (IDA US$3 million + Somalia MPF US$2 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management and Capacity Building
key_components.description: Financing for project management costs including staffing of PCU at federal level and PIUs at municipal level; financial audits; grievance redress mechanism; social and environmental safeguard implementation; monitoring and evaluation including geo-enabled monitoring system; impact evaluation; third-party monitoring; and capacity building in engineering, FM, procurement, safeguards, planning, budgeting, M&E, and citizen engagement.
key_components.financing: US$18 million (IDA US$10 million + Somalia MPF US$8 million)

key_components.description: Allows for rapid reallocation of uncommitted project funds in event of natural or man-made crisis to address eligible emergency needs. No initial funding allocation; draws resources from other components when activated. Requires declaration of eligible crisis and agreement with World Bank before implementation.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Government of Somalia (FGS) through: Benadir Regional Administration (Mogadishu), Garowe Municipality (Puntland), Kismayo Municipality (Jubbaland), Baidoa Municipality (South West State), and future municipalities in Galmudug and Hirshabelle. Ministry of Public Works hosts the federal Project Coordination Unit.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Federal inter-ministerial Steering Committee chaired by Office of Prime Minister and Aid Coordination Unit, meeting quarterly or more frequently. Includes representatives from Ministry of Planning/Investment/Economic Development, Ministry of Public Works, Ministry of Finance, and one representative from each participating Federal Member State. State-level inter-ministerial committees for coordination at state level. Project Coordination Unit (PCU) at federal level in Ministry of Public Works. Project Implementation Units (PIUs) in each municipality reporting to respective Mayors.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank's New Procurement Framework applies. Procurement under special arrangements for FCV countries per World Bank Policy on Investment Project Financing. Prior review of all contracts regardless of value. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents customized to national standards until Somalia's Public Procurement Act is operationalized. Open national competitive procurement for works (Request for Bids). Quality-based selection for consulting services. STEP system used for all procurement transactions. Retroactive financing allowed up to 20% of credit for expenditures from July 1, 2019.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Project monitoring system established by PIUs to assess progress on results framework indicators. Monthly and quarterly reports prepared by PIUs based on supervision agent and project engineer inputs. M&E Specialist at PCU responsible for collating data and quality assurance. Impact evaluation across all four cities measuring socio-economic impacts on different population groups with focus on vulnerable people. Quantitative and qualitative surveys. Continuous iterative beneficiary monitoring and regular social audits. Geo-enabled monitoring system (GEMS) for tracking implementation progress. Third-party monitoring for quality assurance of civil works and safeguard adherence. Baseline and end-line surveys planned.

identified_risks.risk_description: Al Shabaab retains control of territory and is present in urban areas (Mogadishu, Kismayo, Baidoa), collecting rents from major businesses. Fragile relationship between Federal Member States and central FGS with political competition over scarce financial resources. Political process remains unstable.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular communication on project activities, allocations, and processes with all levels of government through project steering committee. Public disclosure of all procurements and awarded contracts. Clear guidelines in Project Implementation Manual for addressing insecurity impacts on civil works.

identified_risks.risk_description: High insecurity across all cities affecting access to locations and staff safety, making monitoring difficult. Security situation may prevent or delay civil works and achievement of project objectives. Al Shabaab activity in urban areas cannot be fully mitigated.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of clear guidelines for addressing insecurity and possibly suspending civil works (outlined in PIM). Third-party monitoring agent to support Bank supervision and oversight. Protection of project workers by security authorities. Flexibility in implementation timelines to account for security constraints.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity in Somalia given history of conflict and mistrust with public institutions. First time municipalities implementing Bank-supported infrastructure investments at this scale. Capacity and authority relatively weak. In Kismayo and Baidoa, municipalities implementing infrastructure investments for first time.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of qualified consultants conforming to Civil Service Commission guidelines. Regular training and support through project management and institutional strengthening component. Intensive support at key implementation stages (bid evaluation, contract management). Engineering supervision consultant with extensive Somalia experience. Capacity building in engineering, FM, procurement, safeguards, planning, budgeting, M&E.

identified_risks.risk_description: High fraud and corruption risks given significant civil works implementation. Weak financial management and procurement environment. Although anticorruption regulations exist, problems persist contributing to low trust in government institutions. Weak PFM legal framework, inadequate FM competencies and internal controls, rudimentary banking arrangements.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strict fiduciary control mechanisms and application of Bank fiduciary rules. Use of direct payments for larger transactions. Designated Accounts managed by Office of Accountant General with ceilings at maximum 2 quarters of forecasted expenditures. Quarterly budget reviews by Bank. Prior review of all procurement regardless of value. Hands-on procurement support from Bank consultant. Third-party monitoring for verification. Disclosure of procurement plans and contract awards. Social accountability measures. Monitoring Agent support for fiduciary challenges. Bank Integrity Vice Presidency portfolio-wide review for fraud/corruption mitigation.

identified_risks.risk_description: High overall environmental and social risks due to insecurity, high GBV risk, and displacement of IDPs. Client capacity to manage E&S risks in complex political, legal, and security circumstances is limited. Project may cause physical and economic displacement of households and structures. Labor and working conditions risks including OHS, child labor, labor influx, SEA/SH/GBV. Risk of exclusion of vulnerable groups. Potential for increased social tensions and violence.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation of Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), Stakeholder Engagement Framework (SEF), and Labor Management Procedures (LMP). Preparation of subproject-specific environmental and social instruments (ESMP, RAP, SEP, LMP) before civil works. Resettlement action plans and labor management procedures. Inclusive and transparent stakeholder engagement. Effective grievance redress mechanisms. Measures to minimize security risks. Protection of project workers. Capacity building for government partners on ESF. Additional Bank task team and supervision consultant support.

identified_risks.risk_description: GBV is widespread in Somalia and endemic in some forms. Intimate partner violence and sexual violence are commonplace. Conflict and displacement exacerbate GBV risks. Sexual exploitation and abuse by people in positions of authority reportedly common. Displaced women and girls at heightened risk. Project-related risks of SEA, sexual harassment, and other GBV forms assessed as high.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust and comprehensive GBV risk management approach. Code of Conduct for project workers with SEA/GBV protections. Community sensitization and awareness raising on GBV risks. Mapping and collaboration with GBV service providers. Reporting and Response Framework for safe, confidential, survivor-centered reporting. Accountability Framework for handling allegations and sanctions. Special channels in GRM for GBV reporting. GBV requirements in bidding documents. GBV expert hired for PCU. Technical specialist for Supervision Consultant. Training of GRM operators on GBV response. Third-Party Monitor to ensure GBV risk monitoring. Regular GBV risk re-assessment. Measures for workplace sexual harassment prevention and female worker safety. Provisions for adequate, separate, safe toilet facilities and safe transport for women workers.

identified_risks.risk_description: Civil works not likely to be complex, but risk rated substantial given implementation through municipalities. First time Kismayo and Baidoa municipalities implementing infrastructure investments. Limited local construction capacity in Kismayo and Baidoa.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: PIUs staffed with Project Engineers. Bank task team includes engineer. Contracted engineering supervision consultant with extensive Somalia experience. Third-party monitoring agent for quality assurance. Use of well-known technologies and methods (asphalt, concrete interlocking blocks, cobblestone). Labor-intensive construction methods. Dedicated supervision consultants for civil works oversight. 20% infrastructure contingency for changes in physical quantities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Somalia's economy has proven resilient but remains fragile with GDP per capita of US$342 (one of world's poorest). Fiscal situation improved but resources for infrastructure limited. Government revenue at 3.8% of GDP inadequate for meaningful service delivery.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project designed to catalyze additional donor funding channeled on-treasury to FMS and municipalities. Use of government systems to strengthen local institutions. Focus on sustainable O&M planning and innovative financing mechanisms.

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertainties remain over assignment of functions to different levels of government pending final constitutional settlement. Deepening rift between federal government and FMS over resource and power allocations. Relations between federal government and States remain fluid.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project includes TA on inter-governmental functional assignments. State-level inter-ministerial committees for coordination. Subsidiary Agreements with each State clarifying roles and responsibilities. Federal Steering Committee for strategic guidance and coordination.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk stakeholder environment characterized by lack of trust between key groups, high contestation and violence. Opposition possible from civil society, private sector, other donors, or districts not chosen for investments. Potential negative impact on PDO achievement.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Key role for municipalities and Project Coordinators in stakeholder communications. Project GRM to manage grievances from communities and private sector. Participatory decision-making process for infrastructure prioritization. Community engagement at all project stages. Transparent consultation processes. Multiple channels for grievance uptake considering low literacy and limited mobility.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism established with multiple channels for uptake considering low literacy rates and limited mobility of women. Special provisions for safe, confidential, and ethical reporting of SEA/SH/GBV incidents integrated into GRM design. GRM operators trained on GBV response. Communities engaged through GRM during implementation. Grievances registered and responded to. World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for project-affected communities and individuals.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender disparities identified across socio-economic and human development indicators. Only 37% of women participate in labor market vs 58% of men. Key project gaps identified: (1) meaningful participation of women in community meetings for subproject prioritization/implementation; (2) involvement of women in construction sector as skilled and unskilled labor. Project actions: Separate meetings with women's groups; female Community Engagement Officers hired; guidelines developed for women's recruitment and retention with protections against sexual harassment/exploitation/abuse; partnerships with vocational training providers for female skilled labor; female-only construction groups if feasible; adequate separate safe toilet facilities; safe transport to construction sites; provisions for maternity leave and nursing breaks; energy-efficient street lighting with climate mitigation co-benefits; women's participation in O&M committees; documentation of lessons learned on gender mainstreaming. Results framework includes indicators on women's engagement in consultation activities and unskilled labor.
vulnerable_groups: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - estimated 2.6 million nationally, 2.2 million in urban areas, 600,000 in Mogadishu, 323,000 in Baidoa
Returnees and refugees - over 88,000 voluntarily returned since 2014; pressure from Dadaab camp returns in Kismayo
Minority groups and those without clan affiliation
Roadside vendors and informal settlers facing displacement

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Theory of change recognizes non-linear and iterative nature in Somalia context. Infrastructure investments selected through participatory decision-making to foster social cohesion and benefit both host communities and displaced populations. Project aims to strengthen government legitimacy through visible, tangible improvements in citizens' lives, critical for sustaining social and political stability. Investments prioritized to integrate IDP settlements into urban fabric and provide benefits to vulnerable groups. Community engagement at all stages to address inequality and exclusion. Project includes TA on informal settlements formalization to address secondary displacement risks from infrastructure upgrading. Measures to minimize security risks and protect project workers. GRM designed to address project-related tensions/conflict early. Strengthened Grievance Redress and Feedback Mechanism with special attention to conflict sensitivity.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia emerging from 2.5 decades of violent conflict and fragility. August 2012 peaceful transfer of power and 2017 handover generated political momentum but fragility remains. Four new Federal Member States created in southern Somalia but federation process complex and challenging. Deepening rift between federal government and FMS over resource and power allocations. Al Shabaab retains control of territory and is present in urban areas. Mogadishu, Merca, and Kismayo named world's three most fragile cities. Urban fragility can have profound impact on Somalia's nascent stability. Rapid urbanization driven by conflict, insecurity, political turmoil, and governance failures compounded by cyclical environmental adversities (drought, famine). 2016-2017 drought displaced 926,000 people; 2019 drought resulted in 3.4 million with acute food insecurity. Large influxes of IDPs may shift clan dynamics potentially destabilizing fragile social structures. Incidence of GBV prevalent, particularly among displaced populations. Cities sprawling uncontrollably with slums increasing and environmental degradation accelerating. Project addresses urban fragility through comprehensive resilience-based approach with five composite dimensions: spatial, physical, economic, social, and institutional.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High insecurity across all project cities affecting access and staff safety. Areas remain in conflict. Security situation may prevent or delay civil works. Al Shabaab activity in urban areas cannot be fully mitigated. Project guidelines for addressing insecurity and possibly suspending civil works to be outlined in PIM. Third-party monitoring to support Bank supervision given security constraints. Protection of project workers by security authorities required. Flexibility in implementation timelines to account for security constraints. Geo-enabled monitoring system essential for inaccessible areas. Community engagement officers to facilitate local coordination. Project design accounts for security risks in staffing, supervision, and monitoring arrangements.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Significant displacement context. Estimated 2.6 million internally displaced persons nationally, with 2.2 million (80% of total) in urban areas. Mogadishu hosts estimated 600,000 IDPs (39% of city population); Baidoa hosts estimated 323,000 IDPs (76% of city population). Over 88,000 refugees have voluntarily returned since 2014. Kismayo under pressure from returning Somali refugees from Dadaab camp in Kenya - 82,840 returned 2015-2018, 64% to Lower Juba/Jubbaland. Baidoa population tripled 2016-2018 from drought-displaced persons (349,000 received). IDPs face increased risks of GBV and sexual exploitation due to limited security in settlements, poor living conditions, limited clan protection. Between January-July 2018, over 204,000 IDPs forcibly evicted across country, more than half in Mogadishu. Project includes specific TA on informal settlements formalization to address secondary displacement risks from infrastructure upgrading. Infrastructure investments prioritized to integrate IDP settlements into urban fabric and benefit both host communities and displaced. Short-term income generation opportunities provided to vulnerable groups including IDPs. Community O&M committees established for project-financed infrastructure. Project aims to create opportunities for IDPs and returnees to participate in public affairs and decision-making pertaining to their own future.
